report_metadata.stage: T3N1M0 (Stage III) with pre-epiglottic space invasion

treatments.name: Concurrent Cisplatin + Radiation (Organ Preservation)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 RCT evidence from RTOG 91-11 (N=547) with 10.8-year median follow-up; replicated across VA Larynx trial. NCCN Category 1 recommendation for locally advanced laryngeal SCC organ preservation.

survival_benefit.rationale: RTOG 91-11: 10-year laryngeal preservation 81.7% (superior to induction 67.5% and RT alone 63.8%, HR 0.46, p<0.001). 5-year OS 55.1%. Standard benchmark for T3N1 supraglottic disease with expected 5-year OS 50-60%.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved, universally available cisplatin 100mg/m2 q21d x3 cycles with concurrent 70Gy RT. Standard at all cancer centers worldwide.

safety_profile.rationale: Significant acute toxicity: Grade 3-4 mucositis 43%, nausea/emesis, nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity. 10-year cumulative grade 3-5 late toxicity 33.3%. Higher non-cancer deaths noted (30.8%). Concern for aspiration in this patient with pre-existing GERD and aspiration risk.

biomarker_match.rationale: Universal applicability regardless of biomarker status. TP53 mutation does not preclude efficacy. Cisplatin sensitivity not biomarker-dependent in this setting.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cisplatin forms DNA crosslinks causing cell death; concurrent administration with radiation exploits radiosensitization through inhibition of DNA repair, cell cycle arrest, and enhanced free radical-mediated damage. 70Gy fractionated radiation causes direct double-strand DNA breaks in tumor cells.
notable_side_effects: Severe dysphagia and aspiration risk (particularly concerning with pre-existing GERD)

treatments.name: Total Laryngectomy + Adjuvant RT +/- Cisplatin

evidence_level.rationale: Level 1 evidence from multiple Phase 3 RCTs (VA Larynx, RTOG 91-11) establishing equivalent survival to organ preservation. NCCN Category 1 for T3N1 supraglottic SCC. Adjuvant CRT supported by EORTC 22931 and RTOG 9501.

survival_benefit.rationale: Excellent local control >90%. 5-year OS 50-65% for T3N1 disease. Definitive surgical approach with highest locoregional control rates. Eliminates aspiration risk entirely via permanent tracheostomy. Adjuvant cisplatin-RT for adverse features (N1 with ECE, positive margins) improves DFS.

accessibility.rationale: Available at all major head and neck surgical centers. Well-established procedure with standardized technique and rehabilitation pathways.

safety_profile.rationale: Permanent loss of natural voice requiring alaryngeal speech rehabilitation. Permanent tracheostomy. Pharyngocutaneous fistula risk 10-20%. Hypothyroidism. However, eliminates aspiration risk which is significant advantage for this patient with GERD and pre-existing aspiration.

biomarker_match.rationale: Universal applicability. Surgical approach is biomarker-independent. Provides definitive tissue for pathologic staging and biomarker assessment to guide adjuvant therapy.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Complete surgical resection of the larynx with bilateral selective neck dissection (levels II-IV) for N1 disease. Adjuvant radiation (60-66Gy) with or without concurrent cisplatin based on pathologic risk features (extracapsular extension, positive margins, perineural/lymphovascular invasion).
notable_side_effects: Permanent tracheostomy with stoma care requirements
Shoulder dysfunction if spinal accessory nerve sacrificed
Psychosocial impact of altered body image

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab + Cisplatin/5-FU (KEYNOTE-048 for R/M Disease)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 RCT (KEYNOTE-048, N=882) with 5-year follow-up. FDA-approved first-line for R/M HNSCC. OS benefit demonstrated across CPS subgroups. NCCN Category 1 recommendation for recurrent/metastatic disease.

survival_benefit.rationale: Applicable in recurrent/metastatic setting: CPS>=20 median OS 14.7 months (pembro-chemo) vs 11.0 months (EXTREME). 5-year OS 16.0% vs 5.2%. For definitive setting, KEYNOTE-412 failed to show benefit when adding pembro to CRT. Scored for salvage utility.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved first-line for R/M HNSCC. Pembrolizumab widely available. Represents standard salvage if patient recurs after definitive treatment.

safety_profile.rationale: Favorable safety vs cetuximab-chemotherapy in KEYNOTE-048. Grade 3-5 treatment-related AEs lower with pembrolizumab monotherapy (55% vs 83%). Immune-related AEs manageable.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient's PD-L1 CPS 15-20 is in favorable range. Maximum benefit at CPS>=20 with pembrolizumab monotherapy. Pembrolizumab+chemo benefits CPS>=1. TP53 mutation may enhance neoantigen presentation.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1) restores cytotoxic T-cell anti-tumor activity. Combined with cisplatin/5-FU chemotherapy for enhanced tumor cell killing and immunogenic cell death. Chemotherapy may increase tumor antigen release and PD-L1 expression, synergizing with checkpoint blockade.
biomarker_requirements: No CPS requirement for pembrolizumab + chemotherapy

treatments.name: Perioperative Pembrolizumab + Surgery + Adjuvant RT/CRT (KEYNOTE-689 Regimen)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 RCT (KEYNOTE-689, N=714) published NEJM 2025. Demonstrated statistically significant EFS improvement (HR 0.76). FDA accelerated approval June 2025 for PD-L1-expressing LA-HNSCC. First advance in resectable HNSCC in two decades.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median EFS 51.8 months (pembro+SOC) vs 30.4 months (SOC alone). 3-year EFS 57.6% vs 46.4%. OS data maturing but trend toward improvement. Applicable if surgical approach chosen for this T3N1 case.

accessibility.rationale: FDA accelerated approval granted June 2025 for perioperative use in PD-L1-expressing LA-HNSCC. Pembrolizumab widely available. Requires PD-L1 CPS assessment (this patient CPS 15-20, qualifying).

safety_profile.rationale: Neoadjuvant pembrolizumab did not affect surgical completion rates. No new safety signals. Immune-related adverse events manageable (thyroiditis, pneumonitis, hepatitis). Added to standard surgical toxicity profile.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient's PD-L1 CPS 15-20 qualifies for this indication. FDA approval specifies PD-L1-expressing tumors. Benefit observed across CPS subgroups but strongest in CPS >=20.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pembrolizumab blocks PD-1/PD-L1 interaction, restoring T-cell anti-tumor immunity. Neoadjuvant dosing (2 cycles pre-surgery) primes immune response against intact tumor antigens and vascular supply, potentially generating systemic anti-tumor immunity. Adjuvant dosing (15 cycles) maintains immune surveillance against micrometastatic disease post-surgery.
notable_side_effects: Surgical complication rates unchanged by neoadjuvant pembrolizumab

treatments.name: Nivolumab Monotherapy (Salvage - CheckMate 141 Regimen)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 RCT (CheckMate 141, N=361) demonstrated OS benefit in platinum-refractory R/M HNSCC. FDA-approved second-line. Established salvage standard of care.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median OS 7.5 months vs 5.1 months (investigator choice). 2-year OS 16.9%. Modest absolute benefit but durable responses in subset. Scored as salvage option after first-line failure.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for R/M HNSCC progressing on/after platinum. Widely available globally. Well-established dosing and administration protocols.

safety_profile.rationale: Favorable safety vs chemotherapy: Grade 3-4 AEs 13% vs 35% (investigator choice). No nephrotoxicity, minimal myelosuppression. Immune-related AEs manageable.

biomarker_match.rationale: Benefit observed regardless of PD-L1 status in CheckMate 141, though enriched in PD-L1 positive. Patient's PD-L1 CPS 15-20 favorable. Approved without biomarker selection requirement.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Nivolumab is a fully human IgG4 anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody that blocks PD-1/PD-L1 and PD-1/PD-L2 interactions, restoring T-cell-mediated anti-tumor immunity in the tumor microenvironment.

treatments.name: Cetuximab + Radiation (Bioradiation)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 RCT (Bonner trial, N=424) published NEJM 2006 with 5-year follow-up. Median OS 49.0 vs 29.3 months (RT alone). NCCN Category 2B for cisplatin-ineligible patients. Multiple confirmatory studies.

survival_benefit.rationale: Bonner: 5-year OS 45.6% vs 36.4% (RT alone). However, subsequent trials (RTOG 0522, ARTSCAN III) showed cetuximab-RT inferior to cisplatin-RT in direct comparisons. Reserved for cisplatin-ineligible patients. Not first-line for fit 45yo patient.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved, widely available. Weekly cetuximab 250mg/m2 (loading 400mg/m2) concurrent with radiation. Available at all cancer centers.

safety_profile.rationale: Better tolerated than cisplatin-RT: no nephrotoxicity, less emesis, less myelosuppression. Main toxicity is acneiform rash (Grade 3 in 17%) and infusion reactions (3%). Lower dysphagia rates may benefit this patient with aspiration risk.

biomarker_match.rationale: EGFR universally overexpressed in HNSCC. No biomarker selection required. Acneiform rash correlates with response. CCND1 amplification in this patient is downstream of EGFR pathway.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cetuximab is a chimeric IgG1 monoclonal antibody targeting EGFR, blocking ligand binding and receptor dimerization. Inhibits downstream RAS/MAPK and PI3K/AKT signaling, reducing proliferation and promoting apoptosis. Also mediates antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC). Concurrent with radiation provides radiosensitization via cell cycle arrest and inhibition of radiation-induced DNA repair.

treatments.name: Induction TPF (Docetaxel/Cisplatin/5-FU) Followed by RT (Chemoselection Protocol)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 RCT evidence from TAX 323/324 and GORTEC 2000-01 trials. TPF shown superior to PF induction in head/neck cancer. Established organ preservation approach with chemoselection (responders proceed to RT, non-responders to surgery).

survival_benefit.rationale: GORTEC 2000-01: 3-year larynx preservation 70% (TPF) vs 57.5% (PF). Allows identification of non-responders for early surgical salvage. VA Larynx: 2-year OS 68% comparable to surgery. 10-year laryngectomy-free survival 28.9% in RTOG 91-11 induction arm.

accessibility.rationale: All agents FDA-approved and widely available. Requires inpatient admission for 5-FU infusion. Available at most academic and community oncology centers.

safety_profile.rationale: TPF adds docetaxel toxicity: febrile neutropenia 5-12%, G-CSF required. Grade 3-4 neutropenia in >75% patients. Sequential treatment may reduce late swallowing toxicity compared to concurrent CRT, potentially advantageous for this patient with aspiration risk.

biomarker_match.rationale: Universal applicability. Chemoselection approach allows early identification of treatment-resistant tumors for surgical salvage regardless of molecular profile. TP53 mutations may predict response to induction chemotherapy.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Three-drug induction: docetaxel stabilizes microtubules preventing mitosis, cisplatin crosslinks DNA, 5-FU inhibits thymidylate synthase blocking DNA synthesis. Chemoselection uses tumor response to identify patients suitable for organ preservation (responders) versus those needing total laryngectomy (non-responders).

treatments.name: Adjuvant Nivolumab + Cisplatin-RT Post-Surgery (NIVOPOSTOP Regimen)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 RCT (NIVOPOSTOP/GORTEC 2018-01, N=680) met primary endpoint of DFS. 3-year DFS 63.1% vs 52.5% (HR 0.76, p=0.034). First superiority over adjuvant CRT in >20 years. Presented ASCO 2025.

survival_benefit.rationale: 3-year DFS 63.1% vs 52.5% (HR 0.76). OS trend favorable but data immature. Applicable if surgical pathway selected for this T3N1 patient with high-risk features (N1 disease).

accessibility.rationale: Nivolumab FDA-approved for recurrent/metastatic HNSCC but not yet approved for adjuvant use. Regulatory submission anticipated based on positive Phase 3 data. Off-label use possible at academic centers.

safety_profile.rationale: Adding nivolumab to CRT increases immune-related adverse events. Grade 3-5 treatment-related AEs higher in nivolumab arm. Manageable toxicity profile but additive to surgical and CRT toxicities.

biomarker_match.rationale: Benefit observed across PD-L1 subgroups in NIVOPOSTOP. Patient's PD-L1 CPS 15-20 is favorable for checkpoint inhibitor response. No mandatory biomarker requirement in trial design.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Nivolumab blocks PD-1 receptor on T cells, preventing PD-L1-mediated immune evasion. Administered concurrently with adjuvant cisplatin-RT (240mg q3w during CRT, then 480mg maintenance) to enhance anti-tumor immune response during and after radiation-induced immunogenic cell death.
biomarker_requirements: High-risk resected LA-HNSCC (positive margins, extracapsular extension, pT3-4, pN2-3, perineural/lymphovascular invasion)
notable_side_effects: Immune-related adverse events (thyroiditis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, colitis)
Additive to cisplatin-RT toxicity (mucositis, dysphagia, nephrotoxicity)
Grade 3-5 treatment-related AEs higher than SOC alone

treatments.name: Induction Pembrolizumab + Docetaxel/Cisplatin Followed by RT (ICoLP/ELOS-type Protocol)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 data from ICoLP trial showing 100% disease control rate and 91.7% larynx preservation rate. ELOS phase 2/3 trial ongoing. Toripalimab + cisplatin/docetaxel achieved 88.9% larynx preservation in Phase 2. No mature Phase 3 data yet.

survival_benefit.rationale: ICoLP: 100% disease control rate, 91.7% larynx preservation. Chemo-immunotherapy induction may improve upon historical TPF larynx preservation rate of 70%. Potential paradigm shift for organ preservation in locally advanced laryngeal SCC.

accessibility.rationale: Available through clinical trials (ELOS trial recruiting). Individual agents FDA-approved but combination protocol not yet approved for this indication. Access primarily at academic centers conducting relevant trials.

safety_profile.rationale: Combines TPF induction toxicity (neutropenia, mucositis) with immune-related adverse events. Manageable in Phase 2 data but additive toxicity profile. Sequential approach may spare late swallowing function.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient's PD-L1 CPS 15-20 is favorable for checkpoint inhibitor component. ELOS trial requires PD-L1 expression. Chemoselection approach retains surgical option for non-responders.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Combines immune checkpoint blockade with cytotoxic induction chemotherapy. Pembrolizumab primes anti-tumor T-cell response while docetaxel/cisplatin induces tumor shrinkage and immunogenic cell death. Chemoselection identifies responders for radiation-based organ preservation versus non-responders for surgical salvage. Pembrolizumab continued as maintenance for immune surveillance.
biomarker_requirements: Locally advanced laryngeal or hypopharyngeal SCC stage III-IVb

treatments.name: Supracricoid Partial Laryngectomy with Cricohyoidopexy (SCPL-CHP)

evidence_level.rationale: Large retrospective series from Institut Gustave-Roussy (N=146) and multiple single-institution series. 5-year OS 71.5-82.2% across pre-epiglottic space invasion subgroups. Not studied in RCTs vs total laryngectomy.

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year OS 71.5% with gross PES invasion, 82.2% with minimal PES invasion. Preserves voice and avoids permanent tracheostomy. However, pre-epiglottic space invasion (T3) and N1 status in this patient may limit candidacy.

accessibility.rationale: Requires specialized head and neck surgical expertise. Available at major academic centers with experienced surgeons. Not universally performed; technique-dependent outcomes.

safety_profile.rationale: Aspiration rate 19% post-operatively; 9% require conversion to total laryngectomy due to chronic aspiration. 4-6 weeks for swallowing rehabilitation. Pre-existing GERD and aspiration in this patient significantly increase aspiration complication risk. Pre-epiglottic space invasion may preclude this approach.

biomarker_match.rationale: Surgical approach independent of biomarker status. Provides tissue for complete pathologic staging and molecular profiling to guide adjuvant therapy decisions.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Supracricoid partial laryngectomy removes the entire thyroid cartilage, both paraglottic spaces, the pre-epiglottic space, and involved structures while preserving at least one arytenoid cartilage and the cricoid. Cricohyoidopexy (CHP) reconstructs the airway by suturing cricoid to hyoid. Preserves phonation and deglutition while achieving oncologically adequate margins for select T3 supraglottic cancers.

treatments.name: Palbociclib (CDK4/6 Inhibitor) + Chemoradiation

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 single-arm trial (N=26) in HPV-negative LA-HNSCC. ORR 41.7% with palbociclib pre-CRT. Biomarker-selected activity: 66.7% response in CDKN2A-altered tumors. Phase 2 abemaciclib trial also reported.

survival_benefit.rationale: Relapse occurred in only 14.3% with CCND1 alterations vs 44.4% without. Patient's CCND1 amplification may confer benefit though response data mixed for CCND1 specifically. Potentially synergistic with radiation via cell cycle arrest.

accessibility.rationale: Palbociclib FDA-approved for breast cancer but off-label for HNSCC. Clinical trials available. Oral agent with manageable administration. Not yet approved for head and neck indication.

safety_profile.rationale: Well-characterized toxicity from breast cancer use: neutropenia (most common), fatigue, nausea. Manageable when combined with CRT. Oral administration is convenient.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient has CCND1 amplification, which is the direct target of CDK4/6 inhibition. >50% of HPV-negative HNSCC harbor CDK4/6-Rb pathway aberrations. This patient's molecular profile (CCND1 amp, HPV-neg) is optimal for this approach.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Palbociclib selectively inhibits CDK4 and CDK6, preventing phosphorylation of retinoblastoma protein (Rb) and blocking G1-to-S cell cycle transition. CCND1 amplification drives cyclin D1 overexpression, hyperactivating CDK4/6; palbociclib directly counteracts this oncogenic driver. Combined with radiation, CDK4/6 inhibition enhances radiosensitivity by preventing DNA damage repair in G1-arrested cells.
biomarker_requirements: CCND1 amplification or CDKN2A loss (optimal responders)
notable_side_effects: Potential additive myelosuppression with concurrent cisplatin

treatments.name: Petosemtamab (EGFR/LGR5 Bispecific Antibody) + Pembrolizumab

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1/2 data showing 67% ORR in 24 evaluable R/M HNSCC patients. Promising early signal but small sample, single-arm design. Phase 3 trials planned or recruiting.

survival_benefit.rationale: 67% ORR represents substantial improvement over historical pembrolizumab monotherapy (17%) or chemotherapy combinations (36-43%). If confirmed in Phase 3, could represent major advance for R/M disease and potential move to definitive setting.

accessibility.rationale: Investigational bispecific antibody in early clinical development. Available only through clinical trials at select centers. Not commercially available.

safety_profile.rationale: Manageable safety profile reported in Phase 1/2. Skin toxicity expected from EGFR targeting. Immune-related AEs from pembrolizumab component. Full safety profile still being characterized.

biomarker_match.rationale: Dual targeting of EGFR (universally expressed in HNSCC) and LGR5 (cancer stem cell marker). HPV-negative tumors like this patient's may particularly benefit from EGFR-directed therapy. No specific biomarker requirement.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Petosemtamab is a bispecific antibody targeting both EGFR and LGR5 (leucine-rich repeat-containing G-protein coupled receptor 5, a cancer stem cell marker). Dual targeting blocks EGFR-driven proliferation while simultaneously eliminating LGR5+ cancer stem cells responsible for treatment resistance and recurrence. Combined with pembrolizumab for triple-hit approach: EGFR blockade + stem cell elimination + immune checkpoint inhibition.

treatments.name: Dalpiciclib + Cetuximab (CDK4/6 + EGFR Combination)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 trial of dalpiciclib + cetuximab in HPV-negative, anti-PD-1-resistant R/M HNSCC published Nature Communications 2026. Novel approach for immunotherapy-resistant disease.

survival_benefit.rationale: Addresses anti-PD-1 resistance, relevant for immunotherapy-pretreated patients. CCND1 amplification in this patient provides biological rationale for CDK4/6 targeting. Preclinical synergy between palbociclib and cetuximab demonstrated.

accessibility.rationale: Dalpiciclib is a newer CDK4/6 inhibitor with limited availability outside Asia. Clinical trial access only for HNSCC indication. Not FDA-approved for head and neck cancer.

safety_profile.rationale: CDK4/6 inhibitor toxicity (neutropenia) combined with cetuximab (skin rash, infusion reactions). Oral CDK4/6 inhibitor is convenient. No overlapping nephrotoxicity with cetuximab.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient's CCND1 amplification is the primary target of CDK4/6 inhibition. HPV-negative status is inclusion criterion. Dual EGFR + CDK4/6 pathway blockade addresses CCND1-driven resistance to EGFR inhibitors.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Dalpiciclib inhibits CDK4/6, blocking CCND1-driven cell cycle progression through G1/S. Combined with cetuximab (anti-EGFR) to simultaneously block upstream EGFR signaling and downstream cell cycle entry. CCND1 amplification drives cetuximab resistance via direct cyclin D1 overexpression; CDK4/6 inhibition overcomes this resistance mechanism.
biomarker_requirements: CCND1 amplification or CDKN2A loss preferred

treatments.name: BCA-101 (Anti-EGFR/TGFbeta Bifunctional Antibody) + Pembrolizumab

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1 data showing 73% ORR in HPV-negative CPS>=20 patients. Very small cohort, early-stage development. Proof-of-concept for dual EGFR/TGFbeta blockade in immunotherapy-responsive HNSCC.

survival_benefit.rationale: 73% ORR in HPV-negative CPS>=20 is exceptional if confirmed. Addresses TGFbeta-mediated immune exclusion which is major resistance mechanism. Survival data immature but ORR signal very encouraging.

accessibility.rationale: Phase 1 investigational agent. Very limited access through early clinical trials at handful of sites. Years from potential commercial availability.

safety_profile.rationale: Early safety data shows manageable profile. EGFR-related skin toxicity expected. TGFbeta pathway inhibition may cause cardiac and musculoskeletal effects. Full safety characterization ongoing.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient's PD-L1 CPS 15-20 and HPV-negative status match the responder subgroup showing 73% ORR. TGFbeta is overexpressed in TP53-mutant HNSCC, providing additional rationale for this patient's molecular profile.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: BCA-101 is a bifunctional antibody that simultaneously blocks EGFR signaling and sequesters TGFbeta in the tumor microenvironment. TGFbeta promotes immune exclusion, epithelial-mesenchymal transition, and treatment resistance in HNSCC. By neutralizing TGFbeta locally while blocking EGFR, BCA-101 reverses immunosuppression and sensitizes tumors to pembrolizumab-mediated T-cell killing.

treatments.name: Adavosertib (Wee1 Inhibitor) + Cisplatin

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1 data (N=10 evaluable) in borderline resectable/unresectable HNSCC. Objective response in 5/10 patients, pathologic response in 6/7 surgical patients. TP53 alterations found in 5/9 responders. Early-stage investigation.

survival_benefit.rationale: Phase 1 data only; 50% objective response rate and 86% pathologic response rate encouraging but small sample. Exploits synthetic lethality in TP53-mutant tumors which cannot activate G1 checkpoint, making them dependent on Wee1-mediated G2 checkpoint.

accessibility.rationale: Investigational agent available only through clinical trials. Phase 1/2 stage of development. Not commercially available. Limited access at major academic centers.

safety_profile.rationale: MTD established at 150mg BID. Dose-limiting toxicities include myelosuppression and GI toxicity when combined with cisplatin. Manageable within clinical trial setting but narrow therapeutic window.

biomarker_match.rationale: Optimal match: this patient has TP53 mutation. Wee1 inhibition is synthetically lethal in TP53-mutant cells that cannot arrest at G1 checkpoint and are forced through G2 with unrepaired DNA damage, leading to mitotic catastrophe. 5/9 responders had TP53 alterations.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Adavosertib inhibits Wee1 kinase, a critical G2/M cell cycle checkpoint regulator. In TP53-mutant tumors lacking functional G1 checkpoint, Wee1 inhibition forces cells with unrepaired DNA damage (from cisplatin) through mitosis, causing mitotic catastrophe and apoptosis. This synthetic lethality concept specifically targets TP53-deficient cells while sparing normal cells with intact p53.

key_evidence.journal: JNCI: Journal of the National Cancer Institute

clinical_trials.title: Pembrolizumab + Docetaxel/Cisplatin Induction for Laryngeal/Hypopharyngeal Preservation
biomarker_requirements: Locally advanced stage III/IVa-b laryngeal or hypopharyngeal SCC
clinical_trials.early_results: ICoLP Phase 2 predecessor: 100% disease control rate, 91.7% larynx preservation rate with pembrolizumab + platinum/docetaxel induction

clinical_trials.title: Perioperative Pembrolizumab + Surgery + Risk-Adapted Adjuvant Therapy in Resectable LA-HNSCC
clinical_trials.early_results: Median EFS 51.8 vs 30.4 months (HR 0.76); 3-year EFS 57.6% vs 46.4%. FDA accelerated approval June 2025.

clinical_trials.title: Adjuvant Nivolumab Added to Cisplatin-RT in Resected High-Risk LA-HNSCC
biomarker_requirements: High-risk features: positive margins, ECE, pT3-4, pN2-3, PNI/LVI
clinical_trials.early_results: 3-year DFS 63.1% vs 52.5% (HR 0.76, p=0.034). First adjuvant therapy to beat SOC in >20 years.

clinical_trials.title: CDK4/6 Inhibitor Palbociclib Before and After Chemoradiotherapy in HPV-Negative LA-HNSCC
biomarker_requirements: CDKN2A alteration or CCND1 amplification (enriched responders)
clinical_trials.early_results: ORR 41.7% with palbociclib pre-CRT; 66.7% response in CDKN2A-altered tumors. Relapse 14.3% in CCND1 altered vs 44.4% without.

clinical_trials.title: Neoadjuvant Nivolumab +/- Ipilimumab Followed by Maintenance in HPV-Negative LA-HNSCC
clinical_trials.early_results: Evaluating neoadjuvant nivolumab followed by maintenance nivolumab +/- ipilimumab vs standard adjuvant therapy.

clinical_trials.title: Wee1 Inhibitor Adavosertib with Cisplatin-Based Therapy in TP53-Mutant HNSCC
clinical_trials.early_results: Phase 1: ORR 50% (5/10 evaluable), pathologic response 86% (6/7 surgical patients). MTD 150mg BID.

combination_strategies.rationale: KEYNOTE-689 demonstrated EFS benefit with neoadjuvant/adjuvant pembrolizumab added to surgery + risk-adapted adjuvant therapy (HR 0.76). For surgical pathway: 2 cycles neoadjuvant pembro, surgery, then adjuvant CRT + pembro maintenance. Immune priming before surgery generates systemic anti-tumor immunity while adjuvant pembro maintains surveillance.

Combining checkpoint inhibition with cytotoxic induction leverages chemotherapy-induced immunogenic cell death to enhance anti-tumor T-cell responses. ICoLP achieved 100% disease control and 91.7% larynx preservation. Chemoselection maintained: non-responders proceed to surgery. Patient's PD-L1 CPS 15-20 supports pembrolizumab benefit.

CCND1 amplification (present in this patient) drives resistance to EGFR inhibitors via direct cyclin D1 overexpression bypassing EGFR dependence. CDK4/6 inhibition blocks this resistance mechanism. Preclinical synergy demonstrated. Phase 1 combination data available. Particularly rational for this patient's CCND1-amplified tumor.

Wee1 inhibition in TP53-mutant tumors causes mitotic catastrophe and release of tumor antigens, potentially enhancing immune checkpoint blockade efficacy. This patient's TP53 mutation and PD-L1 CPS 15-20 create dual rationale for synthetic lethality (Wee1i) combined with immune reactivation (anti-PD-1).

NIVOPOSTOP demonstrated 3-year DFS 63.1% vs 52.5% with adding nivolumab to adjuvant CRT in high-risk resected LA-HNSCC (HR 0.76, p=0.034). For surgical pathway, this patient's N1 disease with potential ECE qualifies as high-risk. Radiation-induced immunogenic cell death synergizes with nivolumab-mediated T-cell reactivation.

supportive_care.approach: Pre-Treatment Speech-Language Pathology Assessment and Swallowing Rehabilitation
supportive_care.evidence: UK National Multidisciplinary Guidelines recommend pre-treatment assessment for all head and neck cancer patients. Prophylactic swallowing exercises during CRT reduce severe dysphagia incidence by 30-40%. Post-treatment rehabilitation requires 4-6 weeks minimum for supraglottic laryngectomy patients.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduces aspiration risk, maintains oral intake, improves quality of life. Critical for this patient with pre-existing GERD and aspiration risk. Prophylactic exercises during CRT preserve pharyngeal musculature and reduce late dysphagia. FEES/VFSS-guided therapy optimizes safe swallowing strategies.

supportive_care.approach: Aggressive GERD Management (PPI Therapy + Lifestyle Modifications)
supportive_care.evidence: GERD is an independent risk factor for aspiration in laryngeal cancer patients. PPI therapy reduces laryngopharyngeal reflux and aspiration events. Elevated head of bed, pre-meal PPI dosing, dietary modifications. Reflux management recommended before, during, and after definitive treatment per multidisciplinary guidelines.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduces aspiration events and pneumonia risk during and after treatment. Particularly important during CRT when mucositis and dysphagia compound aspiration risk. May reduce radiation-related mucositis severity. Essential for this patient with documented GERD and aspiration.

supportive_care.evidence: Tobacco cessation following laryngeal cancer diagnosis predicts response to treatment and laryngectomy-free survival (p<0.001). Continued alcohol consumption post-diagnosis increases mortality risk 2.7-fold (RR 2.7, 95% CI 1.2-6.1). Long-term smoking abstinence >10 pack-years improves OS and cancer-specific survival.
supportive_care.benefit: Direct survival improvement: smoking cessation improves treatment response, laryngectomy-free survival, and overall survival. Alcohol cessation reduces mortality risk by 63%. Combined cessation optimizes radiation response, reduces mucositis severity, and decreases second primary cancer risk from 3-5%/year to <1%/year.

supportive_care.approach: Nutritional Support with Prophylactic PEG Tube Placement
supportive_care.evidence: NCCN guidelines recommend nutritional assessment and consideration of prophylactic gastrostomy before definitive CRT for advanced head and neck cancer. Weight loss >5% during treatment correlates with worse outcomes. Dietitian-led interventions reduce treatment interruptions by 20-30%.
supportive_care.benefit: Prevents treatment-limiting weight loss and dehydration. Ensures adequate caloric intake during severe mucositis and dysphagia from CRT. Particularly important for this patient with pre-existing aspiration risk where oral intake may be severely compromised during treatment. Reduces unplanned hospitalizations.

supportive_care.approach: Dental Assessment and Management Before Radiation
supportive_care.evidence: NCCN mandates pre-radiation dental evaluation. Extraction of compromised teeth before RT reduces osteoradionecrosis (ORN) risk from 15-20% to <5%. Fluoride trays and salivary substitutes for xerostomia management recommended long-term post-RT.
supportive_care.benefit: Prevents osteoradionecrosis of mandible, a devastating complication of head and neck radiation. Preserves dental function and nutrition. Fluoride application reduces radiation caries. Must be completed 14-21 days before RT initiation to allow healing.

supportive_care.evidence: Meta-analyses demonstrate structured exercise during head and neck cancer treatment reduces fatigue by 25-35%, improves cardiopulmonary function, and may improve treatment tolerance. Resistance training preserves lean body mass during CRT-induced catabolism. Shoulder rehabilitation after neck dissection prevents chronic disability.
supportive_care.benefit: Improves treatment tolerance, reduces fatigue, maintains functional status. Particularly relevant for this 45-year-old patient who may return to work. Exercise preserves pulmonary function critical for aspiration management. Shoulder rehabilitation essential if neck dissection performed.
